1: Joab is told the king is weeping and mourning for Absalom. The anointed king’s grief over his rebel son is one of the most theologically charged scenes in Samuel. Christ’s lament over Jerusalem — “O Jerusalem, Jerusalem, you who kill the prophets and stone those sent to you, how often I have longed to gather your children together” (Matthew 23:37) — is the NT counterpart. The king who grieves for those who rebelled against him, whose victory over the rebellion is experienced not as triumph but as loss, is the type of Christ’s heart toward those who reject him. The anointed does not rejoice at the rebel’s destruction.
2: The victory turned to mourning for all the people — they heard the king grieving for Absalom. The paradox of the anointed’s grief at his own victory is the paradox of the cross: the triumph of redemption is simultaneously the deepest grief of the anointed. “He was despised and rejected by men, a man of sorrows and acquainted with grief” (Isaiah 53:3) — the victory of the Servant is accomplished through, not despite, his grief. The people’s victory-mourning at David’s grief is the type of the disciples’ confusion at the cross: the triumph they expected was not the triumph they received.
3: The people crept back into the city like soldiers ashamed after defeat. The reversal of the normal post-victory celebration — returning like the defeated rather than the victors — is the type of the disciples after the crucifixion: “we had hoped that he was the one to redeem Israel” (Luke 24:21), walking away from Jerusalem in dejection. The community of the anointed experiencing victory as loss, triumph as shame, reflects the pattern of the cross where the greatest victory in history was initially experienced as total defeat.
4: “O my son Absalom! O Absalom, my son, my son!” — the sevenfold repetition of grief. The anointed king’s lament for his rebellious son is the type of YHWH’s grief over Israel’s rebellion: “How can I give you up, O Ephraim? How can I hand you over, O Israel?” (Hosea 11:8). Christ’s weeping over Jerusalem (Luke 19:41) is the supreme fulfillment of the anointed’s grief for the rebel people. The cry “my son, my son” anticipates the anguish of the Father as the Son bears the weight of the rebels’ sin — “My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?” (Matthew 27:46) — the deepest cry of the anointed in history.
5: Joab rebukes David: “You have shamed all your servants who today saved your life.” The rebuke of the anointed by his closest military commander is a type of every human attempt to redirect the anointed’s grief toward self-interest. Peter’s rebuke of Jesus — “Far be it from you, Lord! This shall never happen to you” (Matthew 16:22) — has the same structure: the lieutenant who cannot understand why the anointed would grieve rather than triumph. Joab’s pragmatic calculation (stop mourning, reward your troops, or you’ll lose everything) is the worldly wisdom that misreads the anointed’s heart.
6: “You love those who hate you and hate those who love you.” Joab’s accusation is, from the perspective of covenant love, actually a description of Christ’s own ministry: “God demonstrates his own love for us in this: while we were still sinners, Christ died for us” (Romans 5:8). The anointed who loves those who hate him and extends grief over those who rebelled against him is doing exactly what Joab considers incomprehensible. The accusation that Christ “loves sinners” — made by the Pharisees (Luke 15:2) — is the Joab-rebuke applied to Christ. It is meant as an accusation; it is actually the gospel.
7: Joab’s ultimatum: go out and speak to your servants or I swear they will desert you. The political calculation — that the anointed must perform strength or lose his following — is the logic of worldly kingship that Christ consistently refuses. At Gethsemane, Jesus could have called twelve legions of angels (Matthew 26:53); he chose the cross instead of performance. The anointed’s willingness to appear weak, to grieve publicly, to refuse the expected victory-display, is precisely what the Joab-mentality cannot accept. Christ never performed strength to retain his followers; he consistently called them to follow him into apparent weakness.
8: David gets up and sits in the gate — the public resumption of royal function despite personal grief. The anointed returning to his public role after private anguish is the type of Christ’s resurrection appearance: after the grief of the cross and the darkness of the tomb, the risen king appears publicly to his disciples, resuming his shepherding role (John 20:19-20; 2 Samuel 21:15-17). The gate — the legal and public center of the city — is where the anointed is needed; David goes there not because the grief is resolved but because the people need their king.
9: All the people throughout the tribes were arguing: “The king rescued us from the hand of our enemies, and delivered us from the Philistines.” The people debating whether to bring back the anointed king is the type of the human debate about receiving Christ. The argument recognizes his saving acts — “he rescued us” — before the community commits to receiving him. This is the pattern of Acts 2: Peter’s sermon recounts what God did through Jesus (2 Samuel 2:22-36) before the crowd asks “what shall we do?” (2 Samuel 2:37). The recognition of saving acts precedes the decision to receive the savior-king.
10: “But Absalom, whom we anointed over us, is dead in battle. Why are you saying nothing about bringing the king back?” The collapse of the false king clears the way for the return of the true king. The death of the rival anointed — the one the people themselves had elevated — is what opens the question of the true king’s return. Paul’s argument in Romans 10:9: “if you confess with your mouth that Jesus is Lord and believe in your heart that God raised him from the dead, you will be saved” — the resurrection of the true anointed and the collapse of all rival claims is the basis for receiving him. The rebel king’s death opens the throne for the rightful king.
11: David sends to Zadok and Abiathar for the elders of Judah: “Why should you be the last to bring the king back?” The anointed appealing first to his own tribe — Judah — to receive him back is the type of the gospel’s priority to Israel. Paul in Romans 11:11-15 grieves that Israel has not received its own king; Christ himself went first to the lost sheep of the house of Israel (Matthew 15:24). The appeal to Judah to take the lead in receiving the returning king is the type of the apostolic appeal to the Jewish community to be the first to confess the risen Christ.
12: “You are my kinsmen, my own bone and flesh. Why should you be the last to bring the king back?” The appeal to kinship — the same language as the covenant formula of marriage and political union (Genesis 2:23; 2 Samuel 5:1) — is the anointed invoking the deepest covenant bond. Christ’s appeal to his own people through the apostles is the same kinship-claim: “salvation is from the Jews” (John 4:22); “to his own he came, and his own people did not receive him” (John 1:11). The anointed’s “you are my flesh and bone” to Judah is the type of the incarnational claim: Christ’s solidarity with his people as their kinsman-redeemer.
13: The appointment of Amasa as Joab’s replacement — “are you not my flesh and blood?” — is the anointed king extending reconciliation to a former rebel (Amasa commanded Absalom’s forces). The welcoming of the enemy commander into the anointed’s service is the type of Christ receiving former persecutors: Paul, who “was breathing threats and murder against the disciples of the Lord” (Acts 9:1), becomes the apostle to the Gentiles. The anointed’s appointment of former enemies to positions of service is the radical grace that Joab (who will murder Amasa) cannot comprehend.
14: He won over the hearts of all the men of Judah as one man — they sent word: “Return, you and all your servants.” The unified welcome of the returning king by those who had wavered is the type of the eschatological welcome of Christ: “every knee shall bow” (Philippians 2:10). The “as one man” unity of the welcoming community anticipates the unity of the church as Christ’s body: “there is one body and one Spirit... one Lord, one faith, one baptism” (Ephesians 4:4-5). The divided, arguing people becoming unified around the returning king is the ecclesiological miracle that only the anointed’s return can accomplish.
15: The king returned and reached the Jordan — the climactic crossing. The Jordan as the boundary between exile and the promised land is the type of the resurrection-boundary: David returning from Transjordanian exile to the kingdom is the type of Christ ascending from death (the wilderness-beyond-Jordan) to reign. The Jordan crossings in Scripture (Joshua, Elijah, Elisha, John the Baptist) all mark decisive transitions; David’s return crossing is the political type of Christ’s return to reign after his death and resurrection.
16: Shimei son of Gera hurries down to meet the returning king — the man who cursed the fleeing David now prostrating before the victorious David. The reversal of Shimei’s posture — from curser to supplicant — is the type of every enemy who comes to seek mercy from the anointed they previously opposed. “Every knee will bow” (Philippians 2:10) includes those who cursed and mocked Christ; the eschatological kneeling before the anointed encompasses all former enemies. Shimei’s rush to be first — “first of all the house of Joseph” (2 Samuel 19:20) — reflects the urgency of those who know they need pardon most urgently.
17: A thousand Benjaminites with Shimei, and Ziba with his fifteen sons and twenty servants. The large entourage accompanying the penitent Shimei is the type of the communal repentance that the anointed’s return enables. Individual repentance (Shimei) generates communal response (a thousand men). Pentecost is the NT instance: Peter’s proclamation of the returning/risen king produces three thousand converts in a single day (Acts 2:41). The return of the anointed creates a movement of recognition and response that goes far beyond the initial penitent.
18: They crossed at the ford to bring over the king’s household and to serve him. Shimei fell down before the king — the former curser now serving the one he had abused. The service rendered at the Jordan crossing — helping the king’s household over, serving as he wished — is the type of the service of those who come to Christ in repentance: “if anyone serves me, he must follow me” (John 12:26). Shimei’s crossing from curse to service is the pattern of every conversion: former opposition transformed into active service of the anointed.
19: “May my lord not hold me guilty; do not remember the wrong your servant did on the day my lord the king left Jerusalem.” Shimei’s petition for pardon is the type of every penitential prayer before the returning king. The appeal to the king’s mercy — not to hold guilt, not to remember the wrong — is the prayer of every sinner before Christ: “God, be merciful to me, a sinner” (Luke 18:13). The specificity of Shimei’s confession — “the wrong I did on that day” — is the type of honest, specific repentance rather than vague regret. The returning king hears the specific confession.
20: “Your servant knows he sinned.” The explicit acknowledgment of sin — “yāḏaʿ ʿāḇdĕḵā kî ʾănî ḥāṭāʾtî” — is the covenantal admission formula that opens the way to pardon. 1 John 1:9: “if we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness.” The self-identification as “first of all the house of Joseph to come down to meet the king” reflects Shimei’s eagerness to be among the earliest to seek pardon — as the thief on the cross is the first to confess Christ as king and receive the promise of paradise (Luke 23:42-43).
21: Abishai demands Shimei’s death: “Should he not be put to death because he cursed the LORD’s anointed?” The demand for justice against the penitent by the anointed’s own servants is the type of the disciples’ indignation when mercy seems to be given too freely. The disciples rebuking those who brought children to Jesus (Mark 10:13-14), James and John asking to call down fire on the Samaritans (Luke 9:54) — the Abishai-spirit within the community of the anointed. Jesus’s rebuke: “You do not know what kind of spirit you are of” (Luke 9:55).
22: “What is this between you and me, sons of Zeruiah? Why should you be my adversary today?” The returning king refusing to execute justice on the day of his return is the type of Christ’s first coming as savior rather than judge: “God did not send his Son into the world to condemn the world, but to save the world through him” (John 3:17). “Should anyone die in Israel today? Do I not know that today I am king over Israel?” — the day of the king’s return is the day of mercy, not the day of judgment. This is the “acceptable year of the Lord” that Christ proclaims (Luke 4:19), the day when mercy precedes judgment.
23: “You shall not die.” The king’s oath of life to the penitent cursing enemy is the supreme act of covenant grace. The anointed’s sworn word of life — “lōʾ tāmûṯ” — is the type of Christ’s declarations of salvation: “truly, truly, I say to you, whoever hears my word and believes him who sent me has eternal life. He does not come into judgment, but has passed from death to life” (John 5:24). The king’s oath cannot be revoked; Christ’s word of life is irrevocable. Shimei’s pardon by oath is sealed; the believer’s salvation by Christ’s word is sealed with the Holy Spirit (Ephesians 1:13-14).
24: Mephibosheth came down to meet the king — he had not cared for his feet, trimmed his beard, or washed his clothes since the day the king left until the day he came back safely. Mephibosheth’s sustained mourning for the entire duration of the king’s absence is the type of the church’s mourning for the absent king: “we who are in this tent groan, being burdened — not that we would be unclothed, but that we would be further clothed” (2 Corinthians 5:4). The unwashed clothes, untrimmed beard, uncared-for feet are the outward signs of a life oriented entirely toward the king’s return. The church’s “maranatha” (“come, Lord Jesus”, Revelation 22:20) is Mephibosheth’s unkempt waiting, extended across the age of the church.
25: “Why did you not come with me, Mephibosheth?” The king’s question is not accusatory but restorative — he is giving Mephibosheth the opportunity to explain and be fully received back. Christ’s questioning of Peter after the resurrection — “do you love me?” three times (John 21:15-17) — has the same structure: not a gotcha but a restoration. The returning king invites an account not to condemn but to clear the air between himself and the one who loves him. Mephibosheth’s answer will vindicate his loyalty; Peter’s answer will restore his call. The anointed asks so that the covenant person can speak their love clearly.
26: Mephibosheth’s explanation: Ziba deceived him. The lame man who could not saddle his own donkey, who depended entirely on another’s assistance to come to the king, was betrayed by that very servant. The type of those who depend on others to bring them to Christ — and whose access is controlled and potentially blocked by intermediaries — is the type that the Reformation’s doctrine of direct access to God addresses: Christ is the one mediator (1 Timothy 2:5) through whom all come directly, not filtered through potentially corrupt human intermediaries. Mephibosheth’s disability that required intermediation is the type of human spiritual disability; Christ’s direct approach is the answer.
27: “My lord the king is like an angel of God — do whatever seems good to you.” Mephibosheth’s submission to the king’s judgment — placing his entire fate in the hands of the anointed — is the type of complete trust in Christ’s wisdom and justice. “We know that for those who love God all things work together for good” (Romans 8:28) — the believer who says to Christ “do whatever seems good to you” entrusts all circumstances to the one whose judgments are always right. Mephibosheth’s absolute trust in David’s justice — even after being wronged by Ziba — is the model of faith that does not demand its own account but trusts the anointed to see truly.
28: “All my father’s household deserved only death from my lord the king, yet you set your servant among those who eat at your table.” Mephibosheth’s self-description — deserving death, yet given table-fellowship — is the exact structure of the gospel: “while we were enemies we were reconciled to God through the death of his Son” (Romans 5:10). The language of “your servant”, the acknowledgment of deserved death, the wonder at table-inclusion — this is the Christian’s own testimony. The table of the anointed at which the one who deserved nothing is seated is the type of the Eucharist: the “table prepared before me in the presence of my enemies” (Psalms 23:5) at which the redeemed are seated by covenant grace alone.
29: “Why go on talking about this? I have decided: you and Ziba divide the land.” The king’s decision — not perfectly just by earthly standards — reflects the anointed’s prerogative to adjudicate as he sees fit. Mephibosheth’s acceptance of the imperfect judgment is the type of the believer who trusts Christ’s sovereignty even when outcomes are not what they expected. The kingdom’s distribution will not always conform to human calculations of desert; the anointed’s decisions stand even when they puzzle those affected. “Is it not lawful for me to do what I choose with what belongs to me?” (Matthew 20:15).
30: “Let him have it all, since my lord the king has returned home safely.” Mephibosheth’s relinquishment of all earthly claim in the joy of the king’s return is the type of Paul’s assessment of all earthly goods in light of Christ: “I count everything as loss because of the surpassing worth of knowing Christ Jesus my Lord” (Philippians 3:8). The restored covenant relationship with the anointed is worth more than every material claim. The kingdom’s arrival renders the calculation of earthly goods irrelevant — “what does it profit a man to gain the whole world and forfeit his soul?” (Mark 8:36). Mephibosheth gains the king’s return and counts it worth everything.
31: Barzillai the Gileadite had provided for the king during the exile at Mahanaim. The Gileadite who sustained the anointed during his time of need is the type of those who serve Christ in his “absence” — the period of the church age. Matthew 25:35-40: “I was hungry and you gave me food, I was a stranger and you welcomed me” — “whatever you did for one of the least of these brothers and sisters of mine, you did for me.” Barzillai’s provision for David in exile is the type of the care given to Christ’s people during the age when the king is not visibly present. The returning king will honor those who served him in his absence.
32: Barzillai was eighty years old — very old. He had provided for the king at his own expense. The faithful old man whose entire earthly provision went toward sustaining the anointed is the type of those who give extravagantly for the king’s mission. Mary’s anointing of Jesus with expensive perfume (John 12:3) receives the same honor: “wherever this gospel is proclaimed in the whole world, what she has done will also be told in memory of her” (Matthew 26:13). Those who impoverish themselves for the anointed’s cause are honored by the returning king, not repaid in kind but honored in covenantal acknowledgment.
33: “Cross over with me and I will provide for you in Jerusalem.” The king’s invitation to Barzillai to come under his direct provision in the capital is the type of Christ’s invitation to those who have served him: “come, you who are blessed by my Father, inherit the kingdom prepared for you from the foundation of the world” (Matthew 25:34). The faithful servant invited to cross over and live at the king’s expense is the eschatological inheritance: those who served the anointed in his absence are brought into his direct provision in the new Jerusalem.
34: Barzillai’s response: “How many years do I have left? I am too old to enjoy the king’s bounty at Jerusalem.” The old man who has served faithfully but is too near the end to enjoy the full inheritance on this side of death is the type of those who die in faith without receiving the full promise: “these all died in faith, not having received the things promised, but having seen them and greeted them from afar” (Hebrews 11:13). Barzillai’s age prevents his enjoyment of the Jerusalem inheritance on earth; the resurrection is the answer to every Barzillai who served the king faithfully but died before the full kingdom came.
35: Barzillai’s diminished capacity in old age: he can no longer hear the singing, nor taste what he eats or drinks. The physical diminishment that comes with age — the loss of the capacity to receive pleasure — is the type of the gradual wearing away of the present-age body: “though our outer self is wasting away, our inner self is being renewed day by day” (2 Corinthians 4:16). Barzillai’s honest assessment of his condition — no longer able to appreciate what the king offers — is the type of the believer’s honest acknowledgment that the full enjoyment of the kingdom awaits resurrection bodies that cannot wear out.
36: “Your servant will cross the Jordan with the king for a short way. Why should the king give me such a great reward?” Barzillai’s modesty — accompanying the king a short distance and considering that sufficient — is the type of the faithful servant who does not overestimate his own contribution. The parable of the unprofitable servant (Luke 17:10: “we have only done what was our duty”) captures the same posture. The anointed’s honor of such servants goes far beyond their self-assessment — “well done, good and faithful servant; you have been faithful over a little, I will set you over much” (Matthew 25:21).
37: “Let your servant return to my own city, that I may die by the tombs of my father and mother.” Barzillai’s request to die at home — near the graves of his parents, in the city of his inheritance — is the earthly type of the resurrection hope that transcends it. Hebrews 11:16: the patriarchs who died in faith were “desiring a better country, that is, a heavenly one.” Barzillai desires the earthly inheritance; the resurrection hope offers the heavenly city of which the ancestral tomb is only a shadow. But his faithfulness in desiring to be received back to his inheritance is itself honored by the covenant.
38: “Chimham will cross with me, and I will do for him whatever you desire.” Barzillai sends his son in his place — the faithful old servant committing his son to the king’s care. The generational handing-on of covenant relationship — “as I have served the king, let the king receive my son” — is the type of the covenant that runs through generations. “The promise is for you and for your children and for all who are far off” (Acts 2:39) — Barzillai’s covenant service generates a covenant inheritance for Chimham. The anointed king accepts the son of the faithful servant as his own.
39: David kissed Barzillai and blessed him — the covenant kiss and blessing at parting. The kiss of the anointed is the covenant seal; the blessing pronounced over the faithful servant is the spoken covenant favor. Christ’s farewell blessing at the Ascension — “lifting up his hands he blessed them” (Luke 24:50) — is the supreme instance of the anointed’s parting blessing over those who have served him. The blessing that Barzillai carries back to his own land is the type of the blessing that the church carries into the world after the anointed’s departure.
40: The king went to Gilgal with Chimham — the covenant renewal site. Gilgal, where Israel was circumcised after crossing the Jordan (Josh 5), where the kingdom was renewed under Samuel (1 Samuel 11:15), is the recurring site of covenant fresh starts. The king crossing the Jordan and arriving at Gilgal with a new generation of covenant servants — Chimham replacing the generation that served him through the exile — is the type of the church’s ongoing renewal: each generation receives the covenant afresh at the font (the circumcision-type) and the table (the Gilgal-feast type).
41: The men of Israel come to the king: “Why did our brothers, the men of Judah, steal you away?” The inter-tribal dispute over who gets to welcome the returning king is the type of the divisions within the church over who has priority in Christ’s household. The Corinthian “I am of Paul, I am of Apollos” divisions (1 Corinthians 1:12) are the NT instance of the Judah-Israel dispute at the Jordan. The anointed belongs to no faction; the dispute over his loyalty is itself a failure to understand the nature of his kingship. “Was Paul crucified for you?” (1 Corinthians 1:13) — was it Judah’s king only?
42: Judah’s answer: “Because the king is our kinsman. Why are you angry about this?” The appeal to kinship as the basis for priority in welcoming the king reflects the pattern of Jewish priority in the gospel. Paul acknowledges this: “to the Jew first and also to the Greek” (Romans 1:16) — but this priority does not mean exclusivity. The Judah-kinship claim is valid but must not become an exclusionary claim. Christ’s own proclamation “I was sent only to the lost sheep of Israel” (Matthew 15:24) is a Judah-priority statement that is quickly transcended by the Gentile woman’s faith (Matthew 15:28).
43: “We have ten shares in the king, and we have more claim on David than you!” — the Israel-tribes disputing precedence with Judah over the anointed. The unresolved dispute at the end of 2 Samuel 19 sets up the Sheba rebellion of 2 Samuel 20 — the tribal division that follows immediately when the welcome of the returning king becomes a competition. The church’s divisive competitions over priority (who was first, who has more, whose tradition is more legitimate) are the Judah-Israel dispute replayed. “Let us not become conceited, provoking one another, envying one another” (Galatians 5:26) — the anointed’s return should produce unity, not a fight over credit.
